   656→World "FinTuples"
   657→Level 10
   658→
   659→Title "Dropping the Last Element"
   660→
   661→Introduction "
   662→# Fin.init: The Dual of Fin.tail
   663→
   664→You've seen `Fin.tail`, which drops the **first** element. Now meet
   665→its dual:
   666→
   667→**`Fin.init`** drops the **last** element.
   668→
   669→Given a tuple `f : Fin (n + 1) → α`, `Fin.init f` is a shorter
   670→tuple `Fin n → α` that keeps all elements except the last:
   671→
   672→$$\\texttt{Fin.init}\\;f\\;(i) = f\\;(i.\\texttt{castSucc})$$
   673→
   674→The definition uses `castSucc` — exactly the FinNavigation operation
   675→that embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n + 1)` without reaching the last index.
   676→
   677→The duality:
   678→- `Fin.tail` drops the first: `Fin.tail ![a, b, c] = ![b, c]`
   679→- `Fin.init` drops the last: `Fin.init ![a, b, c]` has values `a, b`
   680→
   681→**Your task**: Verify the values of `Fin.init ![a, b, c]`.
   682→"
   683→
   684→/-- `Fin.init` keeps all elements except the last. -/
   685→Statement (a b c : ℕ) :
   686→    (Fin.init (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ)) 0 = a ∧
   687→    (Fin.init (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ)) 1 = b := by
   688→  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
   689→  constructor
   690→  · Hint "`Fin.init ![a, b, c]` at index 0 is `![a, b, c]` at
   691→    `0.castSucc = 0`, which is `a`. Use `rfl`."
   692→    rfl
   693→  · Hint "`Fin.init ![a, b, c]` at index 1 is `![a, b, c]` at
   694→    `1.castSucc = 1`, which is `b`. Use `rfl`."
   695→    rfl
   696→
   697→Conclusion "
   698→`Fin.init` and `Fin.tail` are complementary:
   699→
   700→| Operation | Drops | Keeps | Routes through |
   701→|---|---|---|---|
   702→| `Fin.tail f` | First (`f 0`) | `f 1, f 2, ..., f n` | `succ` |
   703→| `Fin.init f` | Last (`f (last n)`) | `f 0, f 1, ..., f (n-1)` | `castSucc` |
   704→
   705→Notice how the FinNavigation decompositions appear again:
   706→- `Fin.tail` uses **succ** (the `0`/`succ` decomposition)
   707→- `Fin.init` uses **castSucc** (the `castSucc`/`last` decomposition)
   708→
   709→In the next level, you'll see the reconstruction identity for
   710→`Fin.init` and `vecSnoc` — the dual of `Fin.cons_self_tail`.
   711→"
   712→
   713→/-- `Fin.init f` drops the last element of tuple `f`.
   714→
   715→## Syntax
   716→```
   717→Fin.init f    -- drop the last element
   718→```
   719→
   720→## Type
   721→`Fin.init : (Fin (n + 1) → α) → (Fin n → α)`
   722→
   723→## Definition
   724→`Fin.init f i = f i.castSucc`
   725→
   726→## Example
   727→`Fin.init ![10, 20, 30]` has values `10` at index 0 and `20` at index 1.
   728→
   729→## Key identity (taught in the next level)
   730→`vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f` — every tuple equals
   731→its init snoc'd with its last element (`vecSnoc_self_init`).
   732→-/
   733→DefinitionDoc Fin.init as "Fin.init"
   734→
   735→NewDefinition Fin.init

   740→World "FinTuples"
   741→Level 11
   742→
   743→Title "Init-Snoc Reconstruction"
   744→
   745→Introduction "
   746→# Every Tuple = Init Snoc'd with Last
   747→
   748→You know that every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail:
   749→
   750→$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f \\quad (\\texttt{Fin.cons\\_self\\_tail})$$
   751→
   752→The dual identity is:
   753→
   754→$$\\texttt{vecSnoc}\\;(\\texttt{Fin.init}\\;f)\\;(f\\;(\\texttt{Fin.last}\\;n)) = f \\quad (\\texttt{vecSnoc\\_self\\_init})$$
   755→
   756→In words: taking all elements except the last (init), then
   757→appending the last element (snoc), recovers the original tuple.
   758→
   759→The cons/snoc duality is now complete:
   760→
   761→| Build from | Decompose into | Reconstruct with |
   762→|---|---|---|
   763→| Front (`Fin.cons`) | head (`f 0`) + tail (`Fin.tail f`) | `Fin.cons_self_tail` |
   764→| Back (`vecSnoc`) | init (`Fin.init f`) + last (`f (Fin.last n)`) | `vecSnoc_self_init` |
   765→
   766→**Your task**: Prove the init-snoc reconstruction identity.
   767→"
   768→
   769→/-- Every tuple equals its init snoc'd with its last element. -/
   770→Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) :
   771→    vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last 2)) = f := by
   772→  Hint "The theorem `vecSnoc_self_init f` states exactly this:
   773→  `vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f`.
   774→  Use `exact vecSnoc_self_init f`."
   775→  exact vecSnoc_self_init f
   776→
   777→Conclusion "
   778→The cons/snoc duality is now complete. Every tuple can be
   779→decomposed from either end:
   780→
   781→**From the front**: `f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`
   782→**From the back**: `f = vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n))`
   783→
   784→Both decompositions are inverse operations: decomposing and
   785→reassembling always recovers the original tuple.
   786→
   787→This is the tuple analog of a bidirectional linked list — you
   788→can traverse from the front or the back and reconstruct the
   789→whole sequence either way.
   790→"
   791→
   792→NewTheorem vecSnoc_self_init

   799→
   800→Title "ext for Functions"
   801→
   802→Introduction "
   803→# Extensionality for Functions
   804→
   805→In MeetFin, you used `ext` to prove two `Fin` elements are
   806→equal by showing their underlying values are equal. Now you'll
   807→use `ext` in a new context: **functions**.
   808→
   809→For functions, extensionality says:
   810→
   811→> Two functions are equal if and only if they agree on every input.
   812→
   813→In symbols: $f = g \\iff \\forall\\, i,\\; f(i) = g(i)$.
   814→
   815→When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : Fin n → α`, using
   816→`ext i` introduces `i : Fin n` and changes the goal to `f i = g i`.
   817→
   818→This is the same `ext` tactic, just applied to a different type.
   819→In MeetFin, `ext` decomposed `Fin` equality into value equality.
   820→Here, `ext` decomposes function equality into pointwise equality.
   821→
   822→**Your task**: Prove `f = g` given that they agree on every input.
   823→"
   824→
   825→/-- Two functions that agree everywhere are equal. -/
   826→Statement (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h : ∀ i, f i = g i) :
   827→    f = g := by
   828→  Hint "Use `ext i` to introduce an index `i : Fin 3` and
   829→  reduce the goal to `f i = g i`."
   830→  ext i
   831→  Hint "The goal is `f i = g i`. The hypothesis `{h}` says
   832→  `∀ i, f i = g i`. Use `exact h i`."
   833→  exact h i
   834→
   835→Conclusion "
   836→The pattern is simple:
   837→
   838→1. `ext i` — reduce function equality to pointwise equality
   839→2. Prove `f i = g i` for the introduced `i`
   840→
   841→This is the same tactic you used in MeetFin for `Fin` elements.
   842→Both are instances of the same principle: two composite objects
   843→are equal when their components are equal.
   844→
   845→Note: the tactic `funext i` does the same thing as `ext i`
   846→for functions. Both are valid.
   847→"
   848→
   849→/-- `ext` reduces equality of composite objects to equality of their components.
   850→
   851→## For Fin elements
   852→When the goal is `a = b` where `a b : Fin n`, `ext` reduces it to
   853→`a.val = b.val` — two `Fin` values are equal when their underlying
   854→natural numbers are equal.
   855→
   856→## For functions
   857→When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : α → β`, `ext i` introduces
   858→a variable `i : α` and reduces the goal to `f i = g i` — two
   859→functions are equal when they agree on every input.
   860→
   861→## For finsets
   862→When the goal is `s = t` where `s t : Finset α`, `ext x` introduces
   863→a variable `x : α` and reduces the goal to `x ∈ s ↔ x ∈ t` — two
   864→finsets are equal when they have the same elements (extensionality).
   865→
   866→All are instances of the same principle: two composite objects are
   867→equal when their components are equal.
   868→-/
   869→TacticDoc ext
   870→

   872→DisabledTheorem funext
   873→import Game.Metadata

   875→World "FinTuples"
   876→Level 13
   877→
   878→Title "Composing Tuples with Functions"
   879→
   880→Introduction "
   881→# Transforming Tuples
   882→
   883→A tuple `f : Fin 3 -> NN` assigns a number to each index.
   884→A function `g : NN -> NN` transforms numbers. Their composition
   885→`g circ f : Fin 3 -> NN` transforms the tuple by applying `g`
   886→to each entry.
   887→
   888→For example, if `f = ![1, 2, 3]` and `g` doubles its input,
   889→then `g circ f = ![2, 4, 6]` — applying `g` to each entry.
   890→
   891→This is the tuple analog of **map** for lists. It foreshadows
   892→`Finset.image` in Phase 2, where you'll apply a function to every
   893→element of a finite set.
   894→
   895→**Strategy**: Use `ext` to reduce to pointwise equality, substitute
   896→both formulas, then verify each index by case split.
   897→"
   898→
   899→/-- Composing a tuple with a function gives a new tuple. -/
   900→Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (g : ℕ → ℕ)
   901→    (hf : f = ![1, 2, 3]) (hg : ∀ x, g x = x * 2) :
   902→    g ∘ f = ![2, 4, 6] := by
   903→  Hint "Start with `ext ⟨v, hv⟩` to reduce to pointwise equality."
   904→  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
   905→  Hint "The goal has `(g comp f) ⟨v, hv⟩` on the left — the
   906→  composition applied to the index. Use `rw [Function.comp_apply]`
   907→  to unfold this to `g (f ⟨v, hv⟩)`, then substitute both
   908→  formulas with `rw [hf, hg]`."
   909→  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Function.comp_apply, hf, hg]` as
   910→  a single rewrite chain."
   911→  rw [Function.comp_apply, hf, hg]
   912→  Hint "Now case-split on `v` to verify each index."
   913→  cases v with
   914→  | zero => rfl
   915→  | succ n =>
   916→    cases n with
   917→    | zero => rfl
   918→    | succ m =>
   919→      cases m with
   920→      | zero => rfl
   921→      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
   922→
   923→Conclusion "
   924→Composing a tuple with a function gives a new tuple:
   925→`g circ f = ![g (f 0), g (f 1), g (f 2)]`.
   926→
   927→The proof used the same `ext` + case split pattern, but with
   928→an extra step: unfolding the composition (`g circ f` means
   929→`fun i => g (f i)`) and substituting both formulas.
   930→
   931→**Why this matters**: In Phase 2, `Finset.image g s` applies a
   932→function `g` to every element of a finite set `s`. The
   933→composition `g circ f` is the tuple version of the same idea.
   934→Understanding tuple transformation prepares you for set-level
   935→transformations.
   936→"
   937→
   938→NewTheorem Function.comp_apply
   939→

   944→World "FinTuples"
   945→Level 14
   946→
   947→Title "Cons Injectivity"
   948→
   949→Introduction "
   950→# Fin.cons Determines Its Inputs
   951→
   952→You've learned how to *read* a cons'd tuple using the access
   953→lemmas: `Fin.cons_val_zero` and `Fin.cons_val_succ`. Now let's
   954→prove the converse: **if two cons'd tuples are equal, their
   955→heads and tails must be equal**.
   956→
   957→This is the *injectivity* of `Fin.cons`: no information is lost
   958→when you prepend. If `Fin.cons a f = Fin.cons b g`, then `a = b`
   959→and `f = g`.
   960→
   961→**New tool: `congrFun`**. If `h : f = g`, then `congrFun h a`
   962→gives you `f a = g a` — the functions agree at input `a`. This
   963→is the **pointwise extraction** move.
   964→
   965→**Strategy**:
   966→For the head: `have h0 := congrFun h 0` extracts agreement at
   967→index `0`, then simplify with `Fin.cons_val_zero`.
   968→For the tail: `ext i`, then `have hi := congrFun h i.succ`
   969→extracts agreement at `i.succ`, then simplify with
   970→`Fin.cons_val_succ`.
   971→
   972→**Your task**: Prove that `Fin.cons` is injective.
   973→"
   974→
   975→/-- If two cons'd tuples are equal, their heads and tails match. -/
   976→Statement (a b : ℕ) (f g : Fin 2 → ℕ)
   977→    (h : (Fin.cons a f : Fin 3 → ℕ) = Fin.cons b g) :
   978→    a = b ∧ f = g := by
   979→  Hint "Use `constructor` to split into two goals: heads equal
   980→  and tails equal."
   981→  constructor
   982→  · -- heads are equal
   983→    Hint "Extract what `h` says at index `0`:
   984→    `have h0 := congrFun h 0`.
   985→    This gives `h0 : (Fin.cons a f) 0 = (Fin.cons b g) 0`."
   986→    Hint (hidden := true) "`have h0 := congrFun h 0`"
   987→    have h0 := congrFun h 0
   988→    Hint "Now simplify both sides using the access lemma:
   989→    `rw [Fin.cons_val_zero, Fin.cons_val_zero] at h0`"
   990→    rw [Fin.cons_val_zero, Fin.cons_val_zero] at h0
   991→    Hint "Now `{h0}` is exactly the goal. Use `exact h0`."
   992→    exact h0
   993→  · -- tails are equal
   994→    Hint "Use `ext i` to reduce function equality to pointwise
   995→    equality."
   996→    ext i
   997→    Hint "Extract agreement at index `i.succ`:
   998→    `have hi := congrFun h i.succ`."
   999→    Hint (hidden := true) "`have hi := congrFun h i.succ`"

file.content:
Concretely:
- `(Fin.cons a g) 0 = a` — the head of the assembled tuple is `a`
- `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) = g` — the tail of the assembled tuple is `g`

Together with Level 7, this shows that `Fin.cons` and `(head, tail)`
are genuine inverse operations — a bijection between
`α × (Fin n → α)` and `Fin (n + 1) → α`.

**Your task**: Prove both round-trip properties. Use `rw [hg]`
to substitute the hypothesis, then the access lemma or `ext`.
"

/-- Assembling and decomposing recovers the original components. -/
Statement (a : ℕ) (g : Fin 2 → ℕ) (h : Fin 3 → ℕ) (hh : h = Fin.cons a g) :
    h 0 = a ∧ Fin.tail h = g := by
  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
  constructor
  · Hint "The goal is `h 0 = a`. Rewrite `h` using `hh`, then
    simplify: `rw [hh, Fin.cons_val_zero]`."
    rw [hh, Fin.cons_val_zero]
  · Hint "The goal is `Fin.tail h = g`. After `rw [hh]`, the
    goal becomes `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) = g`.
    Use `ext i` to check pointwise, then `rfl`."
    rw [hh]
    Hint "Now use `ext i` to reduce function equality to
    pointwise equality."
    ext i
    Hint "The goal `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) i = g i` holds
    because `Fin.tail` evaluates at `i.succ`, and
    `Fin.cons a g i.succ = g i` by definition. Use `rfl`."
    rfl

Conclusion "
The round-trip is now complete. `Fin.cons` and `(head, tail)` are
genuine inverses:

| Direction | Identity |
|---|---|
| Decompose then reassemble | `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f` |
| Assemble then decompose (head) | `(Fin.cons a g) 0 = a` |
| Assemble then decompose (tail) | `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) = g` |

This means `Fin (n + 1) → α` is in bijection with
`α × (Fin n → α)` — an `(n+1)`-tuple is exactly a head
element plus an `n`-tuple. This decomposition is the foundation
for reasoning about tuples by induction on length.

Next, you'll learn the dual construction: appending an element
at the *end* of a tuple using `vecSnoc`.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 9

Title "Appending with vecSnoc"

Introduction "
# vecSnoc: Adding to the End

`Fin.cons` prepends an element at the *beginning* of a tuple.
What about adding an element at the *end*?

**`vecSnoc f x`** takes:
- A tuple `f : Fin n → α`
- An element `x : α`

and produces `vecSnoc f x : Fin (n + 1) → α` — the tuple `f`
with `x` appended at the end.

The access lemmas are:
- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` — the last element is `x`
- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` — earlier elements are from `f`

Notice the connection to FinNavigation: `Fin.cons` routes through
`0` / `succ` (the successor decomposition), while `vecSnoc` routes
through `castSucc` / `last` (the other decomposition you learned).
These are **dual constructions** — one builds from the front,
the other from the back.

**Your task**: Prove both access lemmas for `vecSnoc`.
"

/-- The access lemmas for vecSnoc. -/
Statement (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) :
    vecSnoc f a (Fin.last 2) = a ∧
    ∀ i : Fin 2, vecSnoc f a i.castSucc = f i := by
  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
  constructor
  · Hint "The goal is `vecSnoc f a (Fin.last 2) = a`.
    Use `exact vecSnoc_last f a`."
    exact vecSnoc_last f a
  · Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use
    `exact vecSnoc_castSucc f a i`."
    intro i
    exact vecSnoc_castSucc f a i

Conclusion "
You now have two ways to build tuples:

| Operation | Adds where | Routes through |
|---|---|---|
| `Fin.cons a f` | Beginning | `0` / `succ` |
| `vecSnoc f x` | End | `castSucc` / `last` |

The duality is exact:
- `Fin.cons` uses the **0/succ decomposition** from FinNavigation:
  every `Fin (n+1)` element is either `0` or `i.succ`
- `vecSnoc` uses the **castSucc/last decomposition**:
  every `Fin (n+1)` element is either `i.castSucc` or `Fin.last n`

This is the payoff from learning both decompositions in
FinNavigation — they power the two dual tuple constructors.
"

/-- `vecSnoc f x` appends element `x` to the end of tuple `f`,
producing a tuple of length `n + 1`.

## Syntax
```
vecSnoc f x    -- append x to the end of f
```

## Type
`vecSnoc : (Fin n → α) → α → Fin (n + 1) → α`

## Element access
- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` — `vecSnoc_last`
- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` — `vecSnoc_castSucc`

## Example
`vecSnoc ![10, 20] 30 = ![10, 20, 30]`
-/
DefinitionDoc vecSnoc as "vecSnoc"

/-- `vecSnoc_last f x` states that `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x`.

The last element of a snoc'd tuple is the appended element.
-/
TheoremDoc vecSnoc_last as "vecSnoc_last" in "Fin"

/-- `vecSnoc_castSucc f x i` states that `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i`.

The earlier elements of a snoc'd tuple come from the original tuple.
-/
TheoremDoc vecSnoc_castSucc as "vecSnoc_castSucc" in "Fin"

World "FinTuples"
Level 10

Title "Dropping the Last Element"

Introduction "
# Fin.init: The Dual of Fin.tail

You've seen `Fin.tail`, which drops the **first** element. Now meet
its dual:

**`Fin.init`** drops the **last** element.

Given a tuple `f : Fin (n + 1) → α`, `Fin.init f` is a shorter
tuple `Fin n → α` that keeps all elements except the last:

$$\\texttt{Fin.init}\\;f\\;(i) = f\\;(i.\\texttt{castSucc})$$

The definition uses `castSucc` — exactly the FinNavigation operation
that embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n + 1)` without reaching the last index.

The duality:
- `Fin.tail` drops the first: `Fin.tail ![a, b, c] = ![b, c]`
- `Fin.init` drops the last: `Fin.init ![a, b, c]` has values `a, b`

**Your task**: Verify the values of `Fin.init ![a, b, c]`.
"

/-- `Fin.init` keeps all elements except the last. -/
Statement (a b c : ℕ) :
    (Fin.init (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ)) 0 = a ∧
    (Fin.init (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ)) 1 = b := by
  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
  constructor
  · Hint "`Fin.init ![a, b, c]` at index 0 is `![a, b, c]` at
    `0.castSucc = 0`, which is `a`. Use `rfl`."
    rfl
  · Hint "`Fin.init ![a, b, c]` at index 1 is `![a, b, c]` at
    `1.castSucc = 1`, which is `b`. Use `rfl`."
    rfl

Conclusion "
`Fin.init` and `Fin.tail` are complementary:

| Operation | Drops | Keeps | Routes through |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Fin.tail f` | First (`f 0`) | `f 1, f 2, ..., f n` | `succ` |
| `Fin.init f` | Last (`f (last n)`) | `f 0, f 1, ..., f (n-1)` | `castSucc` |

Notice how the FinNavigation decompositions appear again:
- `Fin.tail` uses **succ** (the `0`/`succ` decomposition)
- `Fin.init` uses **castSucc** (the `castSucc`/`last` decomposition)

In the next level, you'll see the reconstruction identity for
`Fin.init` and `vecSnoc` — the dual of `Fin.cons_self_tail`.
"

/-- `Fin.init f` drops the last element of tuple `f`.

## Syntax
```
Fin.init f    -- drop the last element
```

## Type
`Fin.init : (Fin (n + 1) → α) → (Fin n → α)`

## Definition
`Fin.init f i = f i.castSucc`

## Example
`Fin.init ![10, 20, 30]` has values `10` at index 0 and `20` at index 1.

## Key identity (taught in the next level)
`vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f` — every tuple equals
its init snoc'd with its last element (`vecSnoc_self_init`).
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.init as "Fin.init"

World "FinTuples"
Level 11

Title "Init-Snoc Reconstruction"

Introduction "
# Every Tuple = Init Snoc'd with Last

You know that every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail:

$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f \\quad (\\texttt{Fin.cons\\_self\\_tail})$$

The dual identity is:

$$\\texttt{vecSnoc}\\;(\\texttt{Fin.init}\\;f)\\;(f\\;(\\texttt{Fin.last}\\;n)) = f \\quad (\\texttt{vecSnoc\\_self\\_init})$$

In words: taking all elements except the last (init), then
appending the last element (snoc), recovers the original tuple.

The cons/snoc duality is now complete:

| Build from | Decompose into | Reconstruct with |
|---|---|---|
| Front (`Fin.cons`) | head (`f 0`) + tail (`Fin.tail f`) | `Fin.cons_self_tail` |
| Back (`vecSnoc`) | init (`Fin.init f`) + last (`f (Fin.last n)`) | `vecSnoc_self_init` |

**Your task**: Prove the init-snoc reconstruction identity.
"

/-- Every tuple equals its init snoc'd with its last element. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) :
    vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last 2)) = f := by
  Hint "The theorem `vecSnoc_self_init f` states exactly this:
  `vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f`.
  Use `exact vecSnoc_self_init f`."
  exact vecSnoc_self_init f

Conclusion "
The cons/snoc duality is now complete. Every tuple can be
decomposed from either end:

**From the front**: `f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`
**From the back**: `f = vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n))`

Both decompositions are inverse operations: decomposing and
reassembling always recovers the original tuple.

This is the tuple analog of a bidirectional linked list — you
can traverse from the front or the back and reconstruct the
whole sequence either way.
"

NewTheorem vecSnoc_self_init

World "FinTuples"
Level 12

Title "ext for Functions"

Introduction "
# Extensionality for Functions

In MeetFin, you used `ext` to prove two `Fin` elements are
equal by showing their underlying values are equal. Now you'll
use `ext` in a new context: **functions**.

For functions, extensionality says:

> Two functions are equal if and only if they agree on every input.

In symbols: $f = g \\iff \\forall\\, i,\\; f(i) = g(i)$.

When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : Fin n → α`, using
`ext i` introduces `i : Fin n` and changes the goal to `f i = g i`.

This is the same `ext` tactic, just applied to a different type.
In MeetFin, `ext` decomposed `Fin` equality into value equality.
Here, `ext` decomposes function equality into pointwise equality.

**Your task**: Prove `f = g` given that they agree on every input.
"

/-- Two functions that agree everywhere are equal. -/
Statement (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h : ∀ i, f i = g i) :
    f = g := by
  Hint "Use `ext i` to introduce an index `i : Fin 3` and
  reduce the goal to `f i = g i`."
  ext i
  Hint "The goal is `f i = g i`. The hypothesis `{h}` says
  `∀ i, f i = g i`. Use `exact h i`."
  exact h i

Conclusion "
The pattern is simple:

1. `ext i` — reduce function equality to pointwise equality
2. Prove `f i = g i` for the introduced `i`

This is the same tactic you used in MeetFin for `Fin` elements.
Both are instances of the same principle: two composite objects
are equal when their components are equal.

Note: the tactic `funext i` does the same thing as `ext i`
for functions. Both are valid.
"

/-- `ext` reduces equality of composite objects to equality of their components.

## For Fin elements
When the goal is `a = b` where `a b : Fin n`, `ext` reduces it to
`a.val = b.val` — two `Fin` values are equal when their underlying
natural numbers are equal.

## For functions
When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : α → β`, `ext i` introduces
a variable `i : α` and reduces the goal to `f i = g i` — two
functions are equal when they agree on every input.

## For finsets
When the goal is `s = t` where `s t : Finset α`, `ext x` introduces
a variable `x : α` and reduces the goal to `x ∈ s ↔ x ∈ t` — two
finsets are equal when they have the same elements (extensionality).

All are instances of the same principle: two composite objects are
equal when their components are equal.
-/
TacticDoc ext

World "FinTuples"
Level 13

Title "Composing Tuples with Functions"

Introduction "
# Transforming Tuples

A tuple `f : Fin 3 -> NN` assigns a number to each index.
A function `g : NN -> NN` transforms numbers. Their composition
`g circ f : Fin 3 -> NN` transforms the tuple by applying `g`
to each entry.

For example, if `f = ![1, 2, 3]` and `g` doubles its input,
then `g circ f = ![2, 4, 6]` — applying `g` to each entry.

This is the tuple analog of **map** for lists. It foreshadows
`Finset.image` in Phase 2, where you'll apply a function to every
element of a finite set.

**Strategy**: Use `ext` to reduce to pointwise equality, substitute
both formulas, then verify each index by case split.
"

/-- Composing a tuple with a function gives a new tuple. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (g : ℕ → ℕ)
    (hf : f = ![1, 2, 3]) (hg : ∀ x, g x = x * 2) :
    g ∘ f = ![2, 4, 6] := by
  Hint "Start with `ext ⟨v, hv⟩` to reduce to pointwise equality."
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  Hint "The goal has `(g comp f) ⟨v, hv⟩` on the left — the
  composition applied to the index. Use `rw [Function.comp_apply]`
  to unfold this to `g (f ⟨v, hv⟩)`, then substitute both
  formulas with `rw [hf, hg]`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Function.comp_apply, hf, hg]` as
  a single rewrite chain."
  rw [Function.comp_apply, hf, hg]
  Hint "Now case-split on `v` to verify each index."
  cases v with
  | zero => rfl
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => rfl
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => rfl
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
Composing a tuple with a function gives a new tuple:
`g circ f = ![g (f 0), g (f 1), g (f 2)]`.

The proof used the same `ext` + case split pattern, but with
an extra step: unfolding the composition (`g circ f` means
`fun i => g (f i)`) and substituting both formulas.

**Why this matters**: In Phase 2, `Finset.image g s` applies a
function `g` to every element of a finite set `s`. The
composition `g circ f` is the tuple version of the same idea.
Understanding tuple transformation prepares you for set-level
transformations.
"

NewTheorem Function.comp_apply

World "FinTuples"
Level 14

Title "Cons Injectivity"

Introduction "
# Fin.cons Determines Its Inputs

You've learned how to *read* a cons'd tuple using the access
lemmas: `Fin.cons_val_zero` and `Fin.cons_val_succ`. Now let's
prove the converse: **if two cons'd tuples are equal, their
heads and tails must be equal**.

This is the *injectivity* of `Fin.cons`: no information is lost
when you prepend. If `Fin.cons a f = Fin.cons b g`, then `a = b`
and `f = g`.

**New tool: `congrFun`**. If `h : f = g`, then `congrFun h a`
gives you `f a = g a` — the functions agree at input `a`. This
is the **pointwise extraction** move.

**Strategy**:
For the head: `have h0 := congrFun h 0` extracts agreement at
index `0`, then simplify with `Fin.cons_val_zero`.
For the tail: `ext i`, then `have hi := congrFun h i.succ`
extracts agreement at `i.succ`, then simplify with
`Fin.cons_val_succ`.

**Your task**: Prove that `Fin.cons` is injective.
"

/-- If two cons'd tuples are equal, their heads and tails match. -/
Statement (a b : ℕ) (f g : Fin 2 → ℕ)
    (h : (Fin.cons a f : Fin 3 → ℕ) = Fin.cons b g) :
    a = b ∧ f = g := by
  Hint "Use `constructor` to split into two goals: heads equal
  and tails equal."
  constructor
  · -- heads are equal
    Hint "Extract what `h` says at index `0`:
    `have h0 := congrFun h 0`.
    This gives `h0 : (Fin.cons a f) 0 = (Fin.cons b g) 0`."
    Hint (hidden := true) "`have h0 := congrFun h 0`"
    have h0 := congrFun h 0
    Hint "Now simplify both sides using the access lemma:
    `rw [Fin.cons_val_zero, Fin.cons_val_zero] at h0`"
    rw [Fin.cons_val_zero, Fin.cons_val_zero] at h0
    Hint "Now `{h0}` is exactly the goal. Use `exact h0`."
    exact h0
  · -- tails are equal
    Hint "Use `ext i` to reduce function equality to pointwise
    equality."
    ext i
    Hint "Extract agreement at index `i.succ`:
    `have hi := congrFun h i.succ`."
    Hint (hidden := true) "`have hi := congrFun h i.succ`"

   508→World "PsetFin"
   509→Level 10
   510→
   511→Title "Modular Equality"
   512→
   513→Introduction "
   514→# When Different Formulas Agree
   515→
   516→Two functions on `Fin 5` are described by different-looking formulas:
   517→- `f i = i + 3`
   518→- `g i = i + 8`
   519→
   520→Are they the same function? In ordinary arithmetic, adding 3 and
   521→adding 8 give different results. But in `Fin 5`, the arithmetic wraps
   522→around: `8 mod 5 = 3`, so `(8 : Fin 5) = (3 : Fin 5)`. This means
   523→`i + 8 = i + 3` for every `i : Fin 5`.
   524→
   525→**Your task**: Prove `f = g` using `ext` and the formula rewrites.
   526→"
   527→
   528→/-- Two functions with the same modular formula are equal. -/
   529→Statement (f g : Fin 5 → Fin 5)
   530→    (hf : ∀ i : Fin 5, f i = i + 3)
   531→    (hg : ∀ i : Fin 5, g i = i + 8) : f = g := by
   532→  Hint "Show the functions agree at every index.
   533→  Use `ext i` to introduce the index."
   534→  ext i
   535→  Hint "Substitute both formulas: `rw [hf, hg]`.
   536→  The resulting equality holds because `(3 : Fin 5) = (8 : Fin 5)`
   537→  by modular arithmetic."
   538→  Hint (hidden := true) "After `rw [hf, hg]`, the goal becomes
   539→  `i + 3 = i + 8` in `Fin 5`. Since `(8 : Fin 5) = (3 : Fin 5)`
   540→  by modular arithmetic, `rfl` closes it."
   541→  rw [hf, hg]
   542→  Hint (hidden := true) "Both sides are definitionally equal because
   543→  `(8 : Fin 5) = (3 : Fin 5)`. Use `rfl`."
   544→  rfl
   545→
   546→Conclusion "
   547→After rewriting, the goal was `i + 3 = i + 8` in `Fin 5`. This is
   548→true by `rfl` because `(8 : Fin 5)` and `(3 : Fin 5)` are the
   549→**same element** — `8 mod 5 = 3`.
   550→
   551→This combines three skills:
   552→- **Function extensionality** (`ext`) from FinTuples
   553→- **Universally quantified rewrite** (`rw [hf, hg]`) from FinTuples
   554→- **Modular arithmetic** from MeetFin Level 19
   555→
   556→**Why does `rfl` close `i + 3 = i + 8` in `Fin 5`?** The numerals
   557→`3` and `8` in `Fin 5` are *the same element*: both reduce to the
   558→element with value `3` (since `8 mod 5 = 3`). Lean computes this
   559→reduction during elaboration, so the two sides are definitionally
   560→equal — no proof step required. This is the power of modular
   561→arithmetic being built into the type: Lean *knows* that `(8 : Fin 5)`
   562→and `(3 : Fin 5)` are identical.
   563→
   564→In a pset, recognizing which skills to combine is the real challenge.
   565→"

   571→World "PsetFin"
   572→Level 11
   573→
   574→Title "The General Decomposition"
   575→
   576→Introduction "
   577→# From Concrete to Abstract
   578→
   579→In the FinNavigation boss, you proved that every element of
   580→`Fin 3` is either `Fin.last 2` or `j.castSucc` for some `j`.
   581→That proof used exhaustive case analysis on all three values.
   582→
   583→Here you'll prove the **general** version: for ANY `n`, every
   584→element of `Fin (n + 1)` is either `Fin.last n` or `j.castSucc`.
   585→
   586→The proof uses a different strategy. Instead of checking each
   587→value, you split on whether `i.val = n` or `i.val < n`:
   588→- If `i.val = n`: then `i = Fin.last n` (by `ext` + `Fin.val_last`)
   589→- If `i.val < n`: then `i = j.castSucc` where `j = (i.val, proof)` (the L13 pattern from FinNavigation)
   590→
   591→The key insight: `i.val < n + 1` (from `i.isLt`) means either
   592→`i.val < n` or `i.val = n`. You can derive this disjunction
   593→with `omega` and then case-split on it.
   594→"
   595→
   596→/-- Every element of Fin (n+1) is either last or a castSucc image. -/
   597→Statement (n : ℕ) : ∀ i : Fin (n + 1), i = Fin.last n ∨ ∃ j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc := by
   598→  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then destructure with
   599→  `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
   600→  intro i
   601→  cases i with
   602→  | mk v hv =>
   603→    Hint "Now `v < n + 1`. This means either `v < n` or `v = n`.
   604→    Create this disjunction: `have h : v < n or v = n := by omega`."
   605→    Hint (hidden := true) "`have h : v < n or v = n := by omega`"
   606→    have h : v < n ∨ v = n := by omega
   607→    Hint "Case-split on `h`: `cases h with | inl hlt => ... | inr heq => ...`"
   608→    cases h with
   609→    | inl hlt =>
   610→      Hint "Here `v < n`, so the element is a `castSucc` image.
   611→      Choose `right` and provide the witness."
   612→      Hint (hidden := true) "`right; use ⟨v, hlt⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
   613→      right
   614→      use ⟨v, hlt⟩
   615→      ext
   616→      rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
   617→    | inr heq =>
   618→      Hint "Here `v = n`, so the element IS `Fin.last n`.
   619→      Choose `left` and prove the equality."
   620→      Hint (hidden := true) "`left; ext; rw [Fin.val_last]; exact heq`"
   621→      left
   622→      ext
   623→      rw [Fin.val_last]
   624→      exact heq
   625→
   626→Conclusion "
   627→You've generalized the concrete decomposition to ALL `n`.
   628→
   629→The concrete proof (FinNavigation boss) worked by checking every
   630→value of `Fin 3`. The abstract proof works by splitting on
   631→`v < n or v = n` — a fact `omega` can derive from `v < n + 1`.
   632→
   633→Notice the **value repackaging** pattern (from FinNavigation L13):
   634→`use ⟨v, hlt⟩` takes the same value `v` and justifies it in
   635→`Fin n` with the new bound `hlt : v < n`. The value doesn't
   636→change — only the type's upper bound shrinks.
   637→
   638→This is the first taste of **generalizing from concrete to
   639→abstract**: the same structural fact holds, but the proof strategy
   640→changes from exhaustive enumeration to case analysis on a
   641→disjunction. This pattern appears throughout mathematics — concrete
   642→examples reveal the structure, but general proofs use different
   643→(often cleaner) strategies.
   644→
   645→(In Mathlib, this decomposition is packaged as `Fin.lastCases` —
   646→an eliminator that lets you handle the last element and castSucc
   647→images separately. You just proved the underlying fact by hand.)
   648→"

   654→World "PsetFin"
   655→Level 12
   656→
   657→Title "The Dual Decomposition"
   658→
   659→Introduction "
   660→# General 0/succ Decomposition
   661→
   662→In Level 11, you proved the general **castSucc/last decomposition**:
   663→every element of `Fin (n + 1)` is either `Fin.last n` or
   664→`j.castSucc` for some `j`.
   665→
   666→The course has emphasized that castSucc/last and 0/succ are **dual**
   667→decompositions — mirror images of each other. But so far, you've
   668→only proved the 0/succ decomposition concretely for `Fin 3`
   669→(FinNavigation Level 10). Here you'll prove it for general `n`.
   670→
   671→The proof mirrors L11's structure: instead of splitting on
   672→`v = n` vs `v < n`, you split on `v = 0` vs `v >= 1`.
   673→- If `v = 0`: the element is `0`
   674→- If `v >= 1`: the element is `j.succ` where `j = (v - 1, proof)`
   675→
   676→**Your task**: Prove the general 0/succ decomposition.
   677→"
   678→
   679→/-- Every element of Fin (n+1) is either 0 or a successor. -/
   680→Statement (n : ℕ) : ∀ i : Fin (n + 1), i = 0 ∨ ∃ j : Fin n, i = j.succ := by
   681→  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then destructure with
   682→  `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
   683→  intro i
   684→  cases i with
   685→  | mk v hv =>
   686→    Hint "Now case-split on `v` itself: `cases v with | zero => ... | succ m => ...`"
   687→    cases v with
   688→    | zero =>
   689→      Hint "Here `v = 0`, so `i = 0`. Choose `left` and use `rfl`."
   690→      Hint (hidden := true) "`left; rfl`"
   691→      left
   692→      rfl
   693→    | succ m =>
   694→      Hint "Here `v = m + 1`, so `i = j.succ` where `j = ⟨m, ...⟩ : Fin n`.
   695→      Choose `right`, provide the witness, then prove via `ext` + `Fin.val_succ`."
   696→      Hint (hidden := true) "`right; use ⟨m, by omega⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_succ]`"
   697→      right
   698→      use ⟨m, by omega⟩
   699→      ext
   700→      rw [Fin.val_succ]
   701→
   702→Conclusion "
   703→You've completed the **dual** of Level 11:
   704→
   705→| Decomposition | General proof | Splits on |
   706→|---|---|---|
   707→| castSucc/last (L11) | `v = n` or `v < n` | Top boundary |
   708→| 0/succ (this level) | `v = 0` or `v >= 1` | Bottom boundary |
   709→
   710→The **value repackaging** pattern appeared again: `use ⟨m, by omega⟩`
   711→takes the predecessor value and repackages it into `Fin n`.
   712→
   713→Both decompositions are now proved for arbitrary `n`. This
   714→symmetry is fundamental: it's why tuples can be built from
   715→*either end* — `Fin.cons` routes through `0`/`succ` (prepend),
   716→`vecSnoc` routes through `castSucc`/`last` (append).
   717→
   718→In Mathlib, this decomposition is packaged as `Fin.cases` —
   719→the 0/succ dual of `Fin.lastCases`.
   720→
   721→There's a unifying concept behind both decompositions:
   722→`Fin.succAbove p` is an embedding `Fin n -> Fin (n+1)` that
   723→'skips' position `p`. When `p = Fin.last n`, `succAbove` is
   724→`castSucc` (skip the last position). When `p = 0`, `succAbove`
   725→is `succ` (skip position 0). Both `castSucc` and `succ` are
   726→special cases of the same 'skip one position' operation. This
   727→means the castSucc/last and 0/succ decompositions are both
   728→instances of a single pattern: decompose `Fin (n+1)` into the
   729→image of `succAbove p` plus the singleton `{p}`.
   730→"
   731→

   737→Level 13

   739→Title "Obtain Meets Decomposition"
   740→
   741→Introduction "
   742→# Combining obtain with Decomposition
   743→
   744→In Level 8, you learned `obtain` for destructuring existentials.
   745→In Levels 11-12, you proved general decompositions that produce
   746→existential results (`exists j, i = j.castSucc`).
   747→
   748→Here you'll combine them: given that an element is a `castSucc`
   749→image, use `obtain` to extract the preimage, then prove the
   750→element can't be `Fin.last`.
   751→
   752→This is the **converse** of FinNavigation Level 14 (surjectivity):
   753→there you proved `i /= last -> exists j, i = j.castSucc`. Here
   754→you prove `exists j, i = j.castSucc -> i /= last`.
   755→
   756→**Strategy**:
   757→1. `obtain` the witness from the existential
   758→2. Rewrite using the equation
   759→3. Apply the value-reasoning pattern from FinNavigation L07
   760→"
   761→
   762→/-- A castSucc image is never the last element. -/
   763→Statement (n : ℕ) (i : Fin (n + 1)) (h : ∃ j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc) :
   764→    i ≠ Fin.last n := by
   765→  Hint "Destructure the existential: `obtain ⟨j, hj⟩ := h`.
   766→  This gives you the preimage `j` and the equation `hj`."
   767→  obtain ⟨j, hj⟩ := h
   768→  Hint "Rewrite `i` using the equation: `rw [hj]`.
   769→  Now the goal is `j.castSucc /= Fin.last n`."
   770→  rw [hj]
   771→  Hint "This is the separation fact from FinNavigation L07, now
   772→  for general `n`. Assume equality and derive a contradiction
   773→  via value reasoning."
   774→  Hint (hidden := true) "`intro heq; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at heq;
   775→  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at heq; omega`"
   776→  intro heq
   777→  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at heq
   778→  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at heq
   779→  omega
   780→
   781→Conclusion "
   782→You combined two skills:
   783→1. **`obtain`** to destructure the existential hypothesis
   784→2. **Value reasoning** (ext_iff + val lemmas + omega) to derive
   785→   the contradiction
   786→
   787→This is a common pattern in real Lean proofs: a previous result
   788→gives you an existential (`exists j, i = j.castSucc`), and you
   789→need to destructure it with `obtain` before you can use the
   790→equation. The value repackaging goes in reverse here — instead
   791→of constructing a witness, you're unpacking one that was given.
   792→
   793→The fact you just proved — castSucc images are never last — is
   794→the converse of the surjectivity result from FinNavigation L14:
   795→non-last elements are always castSucc images. Together they say:
   796→an element is a castSucc image *if and only if* it's not last.
   797→"

   803→World "PsetFin"
   804→Level 14
   805→
   806→Title "A Cyclic Permutation"
   807→
   808→Introduction "
   809→# Permutations as Functions
   810→
   811→A **permutation** of `Fin n` is a bijection `Fin n -> Fin n` —
   812→a rearrangement of all the elements. Permutations are fundamental
   813→objects in algebra (they form the symmetric group $S_n$) and in
   814→combinatorics (counting arrangements).
   815→
   816→The simplest permutation is the **identity**: every element maps to
   817→itself. Every element is a fixed point. The simplest *nontrivial*
   818→permutation of `Fin 3` is the **cyclic permutation**:
   819→$0 \\mapsto 1 \\mapsto 2 \\mapsto 0$. In Lean, this is the tuple
   820→`![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 -> Fin 3`.
   821→
   822→**Your task**: Prove that this permutation has **no fixed points**
   823→— no element maps to itself. This requires checking all three
   824→indices with the case split + inequality pattern.
   825→"
   826→
   827→/-- The cyclic permutation on Fin 3 has no fixed points. -/
   828→Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 → Fin 3) i ≠ i := by
   829→  Hint "Destructure `i` and case-split on all three values.
   830→  At each value, the permutation's output differs from the input."
   831→  Hint (hidden := true) "`intro i; cases i with | mk v hv =>`
   832→  then `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
   833→  intro i
   834→  cases i with
   835→  | mk v hv =>
   836→    cases v with
   837→    | zero =>
   838→      Hint "Here `i = 0` and `![1, 2, 0] 0 = 1`. Prove `1 /= 0`."
   839→      Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h; cases h`"
   840→      intro h; cases h
   841→    | succ n =>
   842→      cases n with
   843→      | zero =>
   844→        Hint "Here `i = 1` and `![1, 2, 0] 1 = 2`. Prove `2 /= 1`."
   845→        Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h; cases h`"
   846→        intro h; cases h
   847→      | succ m =>
   848→        cases m with
   849→        | zero =>
   850→          Hint "Here `i = 2` and `![1, 2, 0] 2 = 0`. Prove `0 /= 2`."
   851→          Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h; cases h`"
   852→          intro h; cases h
   853→        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
   854→
   855→Conclusion "
   856→The cyclic permutation `![1, 2, 0]` sends every element to a
   857→different element — it has no fixed points. A permutation with
   858→no fixed points is called a **derangement**. Derangements appear
   859→throughout combinatorics — counting them leads to beautiful
   860→formulas involving alternating sums and factorials.
   861→
   862→This is your first encounter with `Fin n -> Fin n` as an
   863→algebraic object. In later worlds, you'll see that:
   864→- Permutations form a **group** under composition
   865→- The number of permutations of `Fin n` is $n!$ (factorial)
   866→- The number of derangements of `Fin n` is approximately $n!/e$
   867→
   868→The tools you already have — case analysis, inequality, function
   869→extensionality — are exactly what you need to prove properties
   870→of concrete permutations.
   871→"

   877→World "PsetFin"
   878→Level 15
   879→
   880→Title "Permutation Algebra"
   881→
   882→Introduction "
   883→# Composition and Inverse
   884→
   885→In Level 14, you proved that the cyclic permutation `![1, 2, 0]`
   886→has no fixed points. Now you'll study its **algebraic structure**:
   887→what happens when you compose it with itself?
   888→
   889→**Part 1 — Composition**: Applying the cyclic permutation twice gives
   890→the **reverse cycle** `![2, 0, 1]`:
   891→- `0 -> 1 -> 2`
   892→- `1 -> 2 -> 0`
   893→- `2 -> 0 -> 1`
   894→
   895→**Part 2 — Inverse**: The reverse cycle `![2, 0, 1]` is the
   896→**inverse** of `![1, 2, 0]` — composing them gives the identity:
   897→- `0 -> 1 -> 0`
   898→- `1 -> 2 -> 1`
   899→- `2 -> 0 -> 2`
   900→
   901→Each element returns to itself!
   902→
   903→**Strategy** for both parts: `ext`, unfold with `Function.comp_apply`,
   904→case-split on the index, and close with `rfl`.
   905→"
   906→
   907→/-- The square of the cyclic permutation and its inverse property. -/
   908→Statement : ((![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 → Fin 3) ∘ (![1, 2, 0]) = ![2, 0, 1]) ∧
   909→            ((![2, 0, 1] : Fin 3 → Fin 3) ∘ (![1, 2, 0]) = id) := by
   910→  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it."
   911→  constructor
   912→  -- Part 1: Composition gives reverse cycle
   913→  · Hint "Part 1: Prove the square is `![2, 0, 1]`.
   914→    Use `ext` to reduce to pointwise equality, then unfold
   915→    the composition with `Function.comp_apply` and case-split."
   916→    ext ⟨v, hv⟩
   917→    Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Function.comp_apply]` unfolds the
   918→    composition. Then case-split on `v`."
   919→    rw [Function.comp_apply]
   920→    cases v with
   921→    | zero =>
   922→      Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl` — at index 0: `![1,2,0] (![1,2,0] 0) = ![1,2,0] 1 = 2`."
   923→      rfl
   924→    | succ n =>
   925→      cases n with
   926→      | zero =>
   927→        Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl` — at index 1: `![1,2,0] (![1,2,0] 1) = ![1,2,0] 2 = 0`."
   928→        rfl
   929→      | succ m =>
   930→        cases m with
   931→        | zero =>
   932→          Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl` — at index 2: `![1,2,0] (![1,2,0] 2) = ![1,2,0] 0 = 1`."
   933→          rfl
   934→        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
   935→  -- Part 2: Reverse cycle is the inverse
   936→  · Hint "Part 2: Prove `![2, 0, 1]` composed with `![1, 2, 0]` is `id`.
   937→    Same pattern: `ext`, unfold, case-split."
   938→    ext ⟨v, hv⟩
   939→    Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Function.comp_apply]` then case-split."
   940→    rw [Function.comp_apply]
   941→    cases v with
   942→    | zero =>
   943→      Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl` — `![2,0,1] (![1,2,0] 0) = ![2,0,1] 1 = 0 = id 0`."
   944→      rfl
   945→    | succ n =>
   946→      cases n with
   947→      | zero =>
   948→        Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl` — `![2,0,1] (![1,2,0] 1) = ![2,0,1] 2 = 1 = id 1`."
   949→        rfl
   950→      | succ m =>
   951→        cases m with
   952→        | zero =>
   953→          Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl` — `![2,0,1] (![1,2,0] 2) = ![2,0,1] 0 = 2 = id 2`."
   954→          rfl
   955→        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
   956→
   957→Conclusion "
   958→You've proved both that the cyclic permutation squared gives
   959→`![2, 0, 1]`, and that `![2, 0, 1]` is its **inverse**: composing
   960→them gives the identity.
   961→
   962→This is a surprising fact about order-3 elements: the square of an
   963→order-3 permutation IS its inverse. Why? If `f^3 = id`, then
   964→`f^2 . f = id`, so `f^2 = f^(-1)`. Squaring an order-3 element
   965→doesn't move you further from the identity — it brings you one
   966→step closer.
   967→
   968→In group theory, this generalizes: for any element `g` of order `n`,
   969→`g^(-1) = g^(n-1)`. You'll verify the order-3 fact directly in the
   970→next level.
   971→"
   972→

   977→World "PsetFin"
   978→Level 16
   979→
   980→Title "The Cyclic Permutation Has Order 3"
   981→
   982→Introduction "
   983→# Proving Order 3 Directly
   984→
   985→In Level 15, you proved `![1,2,0]^2 = ![2,0,1]` and that
   986→`![2,0,1]` is the inverse (composing it with `![1,2,0]` gives `id`).
   987→
   988→Now prove the punchline directly: applying `![1,2,0]` **three
   989→times** returns to the identity.
   990→
   991→The composition `![1,2,0] comp ![1,2,0] comp ![1,2,0]` evaluates as:
   992→- At 0: `0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 0`
   993→- At 1: `1 -> 2 -> 0 -> 1`
   994→- At 2: `2 -> 0 -> 1 -> 2`
   995→
   996→Every element returns to itself after three steps.
   997→
   998→**Strategy**: `ext`, then `rw [Function.comp_apply, Function.comp_apply]`
   999→to unfold both layers of composition, then case-split and close with `rfl`.

World "PsetFin"
Level 10

Title "Modular Equality"

Introduction "
# When Different Formulas Agree

Two functions on `Fin 5` are described by different-looking formulas:
- `f i = i + 3`
- `g i = i + 8`

Are they the same function? In ordinary arithmetic, adding 3 and
adding 8 give different results. But in `Fin 5`, the arithmetic wraps
around: `8 mod 5 = 3`, so `(8 : Fin 5) = (3 : Fin 5)`. This means
`i + 8 = i + 3` for every `i : Fin 5`.

**Your task**: Prove `f = g` using `ext` and the formula rewrites.
"

/-- Two functions with the same modular formula are equal. -/
Statement (f g : Fin 5 → Fin 5)
    (hf : ∀ i : Fin 5, f i = i + 3)
    (hg : ∀ i : Fin 5, g i = i + 8) : f = g := by
  Hint "Show the functions agree at every index.
  Use `ext i` to introduce the index."
  ext i
  Hint "Substitute both formulas: `rw [hf, hg]`.
  The resulting equality holds because `(3 : Fin 5) = (8 : Fin 5)`
  by modular arithmetic."
  Hint (hidden := true) "After `rw [hf, hg]`, the goal becomes
  `i + 3 = i + 8` in `Fin 5`. Since `(8 : Fin 5) = (3 : Fin 5)`
  by modular arithmetic, `rfl` closes it."
  rw [hf, hg]
  Hint (hidden := true) "Both sides are definitionally equal because
  `(8 : Fin 5) = (3 : Fin 5)`. Use `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
After rewriting, the goal was `i + 3 = i + 8` in `Fin 5`. This is
true by `rfl` because `(8 : Fin 5)` and `(3 : Fin 5)` are the
**same element** — `8 mod 5 = 3`.

This combines three skills:
- **Function extensionality** (`ext`) from FinTuples
- **Universally quantified rewrite** (`rw [hf, hg]`) from FinTuples
- **Modular arithmetic** from MeetFin Level 19

**Why does `rfl` close `i + 3 = i + 8` in `Fin 5`?** The numerals
`3` and `8` in `Fin 5` are *the same element*: both reduce to the
element with value `3` (since `8 mod 5 = 3`). Lean computes this
reduction during elaboration, so the two sides are definitionally
equal — no proof step required. This is the power of modular
arithmetic being built into the type: Lean *knows* that `(8 : Fin 5)`
and `(3 : Fin 5)` are identical.

In a pset, recognizing which skills to combine is the real challenge.
"

World "PsetFin"
Level 11

Title "The General Decomposition"

Introduction "
# From Concrete to Abstract

In the FinNavigation boss, you proved that every element of
`Fin 3` is either `Fin.last 2` or `j.castSucc` for some `j`.
That proof used exhaustive case analysis on all three values.

Here you'll prove the **general** version: for ANY `n`, every
element of `Fin (n + 1)` is either `Fin.last n` or `j.castSucc`.

The proof uses a different strategy. Instead of checking each
value, you split on whether `i.val = n` or `i.val < n`:
- If `i.val = n`: then `i = Fin.last n` (by `ext` + `Fin.val_last`)
- If `i.val < n`: then `i = j.castSucc` where `j = (i.val, proof)` (the L13 pattern from FinNavigation)

The key insight: `i.val < n + 1` (from `i.isLt`) means either
`i.val < n` or `i.val = n`. You can derive this disjunction
with `omega` and then case-split on it.
"

/-- Every element of Fin (n+1) is either last or a castSucc image. -/
Statement (n : ℕ) : ∀ i : Fin (n + 1), i = Fin.last n ∨ ∃ j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then destructure with
  `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
  intro i
  cases i with
  | mk v hv =>
    Hint "Now `v < n + 1`. This means either `v < n` or `v = n`.
    Create this disjunction: `have h : v < n or v = n := by omega`."
    Hint (hidden := true) "`have h : v < n or v = n := by omega`"
    have h : v < n ∨ v = n := by omega
    Hint "Case-split on `h`: `cases h with | inl hlt => ... | inr heq => ...`"
    cases h with
    | inl hlt =>
      Hint "Here `v < n`, so the element is a `castSucc` image.
      Choose `right` and provide the witness."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`right; use ⟨v, hlt⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
      right
      use ⟨v, hlt⟩
      ext
      rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
    | inr heq =>
      Hint "Here `v = n`, so the element IS `Fin.last n`.
      Choose `left` and prove the equality."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`left; ext; rw [Fin.val_last]; exact heq`"
      left
      ext
      rw [Fin.val_last]
      exact heq

Conclusion "
You've generalized the concrete decomposition to ALL `n`.

The concrete proof (FinNavigation boss) worked by checking every
value of `Fin 3`. The abstract proof works by splitting on
`v < n or v = n` — a fact `omega` can derive from `v < n + 1`.

Notice the **value repackaging** pattern (from FinNavigation L13):
`use ⟨v, hlt⟩` takes the same value `v` and justifies it in
`Fin n` with the new bound `hlt : v < n`. The value doesn't
change — only the type's upper bound shrinks.

This is the first taste of **generalizing from concrete to
abstract**: the same structural fact holds, but the proof strategy
changes from exhaustive enumeration to case analysis on a
disjunction. This pattern appears throughout mathematics — concrete
examples reveal the structure, but general proofs use different
(often cleaner) strategies.

(In Mathlib, this decomposition is packaged as `Fin.lastCases` —
an eliminator that lets you handle the last element and castSucc
images separately. You just proved the underlying fact by hand.)
"

World "PsetFin"
Level 12

Title "The Dual Decomposition"

Introduction "
# General 0/succ Decomposition

In Level 11, you proved the general **castSucc/last decomposition**:
every element of `Fin (n + 1)` is either `Fin.last n` or
`j.castSucc` for some `j`.

The course has emphasized that castSucc/last and 0/succ are **dual**
decompositions — mirror images of each other. But so far, you've
only proved the 0/succ decomposition concretely for `Fin 3`
(FinNavigation Level 10). Here you'll prove it for general `n`.

The proof mirrors L11's structure: instead of splitting on
`v = n` vs `v < n`, you split on `v = 0` vs `v >= 1`.
- If `v = 0`: the element is `0`
- If `v >= 1`: the element is `j.succ` where `j = (v - 1, proof)`

**Your task**: Prove the general 0/succ decomposition.
"

/-- Every element of Fin (n+1) is either 0 or a successor. -/
Statement (n : ℕ) : ∀ i : Fin (n + 1), i = 0 ∨ ∃ j : Fin n, i = j.succ := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then destructure with
  `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
  intro i
  cases i with
  | mk v hv =>
    Hint "Now case-split on `v` itself: `cases v with | zero => ... | succ m => ...`"
    cases v with
    | zero =>
      Hint "Here `v = 0`, so `i = 0`. Choose `left` and use `rfl`."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`left; rfl`"
      left
      rfl
    | succ m =>
      Hint "Here `v = m + 1`, so `i = j.succ` where `j = ⟨m, ...⟩ : Fin n`.
      Choose `right`, provide the witness, then prove via `ext` + `Fin.val_succ`."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`right; use ⟨m, by omega⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_succ]`"
      right
      use ⟨m, by omega⟩
      ext
      rw [Fin.val_succ]

Conclusion "
You've completed the **dual** of Level 11:

| Decomposition | General proof | Splits on |
|---|---|---|
| castSucc/last (L11) | `v = n` or `v < n` | Top boundary |
| 0/succ (this level) | `v = 0` or `v >= 1` | Bottom boundary |

The **value repackaging** pattern appeared again: `use ⟨m, by omega⟩`
takes the predecessor value and repackages it into `Fin n`.

Both decompositions are now proved for arbitrary `n`. This
symmetry is fundamental: it's why tuples can be built from
*either end* — `Fin.cons` routes through `0`/`succ` (prepend),
`vecSnoc` routes through `castSucc`/`last` (append).

In Mathlib, this decomposition is packaged as `Fin.cases` —
the 0/succ dual of `Fin.lastCases`.

There's a unifying concept behind both decompositions:
`Fin.succAbove p` is an embedding `Fin n -> Fin (n+1)` that
'skips' position `p`. When `p = Fin.last n`, `succAbove` is
`castSucc` (skip the last position). When `p = 0`, `succAbove`
is `succ` (skip position 0). Both `castSucc` and `succ` are
special cases of the same 'skip one position' operation. This
means the castSucc/last and 0/succ decompositions are both
instances of a single pattern: decompose `Fin (n+1)` into the
image of `succAbove p` plus the singleton `{p}`.
"

World "PsetFin"
Level 13

Title "Obtain Meets Decomposition"

Introduction "
# Combining obtain with Decomposition

In Level 8, you learned `obtain` for destructuring existentials.
In Levels 11-12, you proved general decompositions that produce
existential results (`exists j, i = j.castSucc`).

Here you'll combine them: given that an element is a `castSucc`
image, use `obtain` to extract the preimage, then prove the
element can't be `Fin.last`.

This is the **converse** of FinNavigation Level 14 (surjectivity):
there you proved `i /= last -> exists j, i = j.castSucc`. Here
you prove `exists j, i = j.castSucc -> i /= last`.

**Strategy**:
1. `obtain` the witness from the existential
2. Rewrite using the equation
3. Apply the value-reasoning pattern from FinNavigation L07
"

/-- A castSucc image is never the last element. -/
Statement (n : ℕ) (i : Fin (n + 1)) (h : ∃ j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc) :
    i ≠ Fin.last n := by
  Hint "Destructure the existential: `obtain ⟨j, hj⟩ := h`.
  This gives you the preimage `j` and the equation `hj`."
  obtain ⟨j, hj⟩ := h
  Hint "Rewrite `i` using the equation: `rw [hj]`.
  Now the goal is `j.castSucc /= Fin.last n`."
  rw [hj]
  Hint "This is the separation fact from FinNavigation L07, now
  for general `n`. Assume equality and derive a contradiction
  via value reasoning."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`intro heq; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at heq;
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at heq; omega`"
  intro heq
  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at heq
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at heq
  omega

Conclusion "
You combined two skills:
1. **`obtain`** to destructure the existential hypothesis
2. **Value reasoning** (ext_iff + val lemmas + omega) to derive
   the contradiction

This is a common pattern in real Lean proofs: a previous result
gives you an existential (`exists j, i = j.castSucc`), and you
need to destructure it with `obtain` before you can use the
equation. The value repackaging goes in reverse here — instead
of constructing a witness, you're unpacking one that was given.

The fact you just proved — castSucc images are never last — is
the converse of the surjectivity result from FinNavigation L14:
non-last elements are always castSucc images. Together they say:
an element is a castSucc image *if and only if* it's not last.
"

World "PsetFin"
Level 14

Title "A Cyclic Permutation"

Introduction "
# Permutations as Functions

A **permutation** of `Fin n` is a bijection `Fin n -> Fin n` —
a rearrangement of all the elements. Permutations are fundamental
objects in algebra (they form the symmetric group $S_n$) and in
combinatorics (counting arrangements).

The simplest permutation is the **identity**: every element maps to
itself. Every element is a fixed point. The simplest *nontrivial*
permutation of `Fin 3` is the **cyclic permutation**:
$0 \\mapsto 1 \\mapsto 2 \\mapsto 0$. In Lean, this is the tuple
`![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 -> Fin 3`.

**Your task**: Prove that this permutation has **no fixed points**
— no element maps to itself. This requires checking all three
indices with the case split + inequality pattern.
"

/-- The cyclic permutation on Fin 3 has no fixed points. -/
Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 → Fin 3) i ≠ i := by
  Hint "Destructure `i` and case-split on all three values.
  At each value, the permutation's output differs from the input."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`intro i; cases i with | mk v hv =>`
  then `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
  intro i
  cases i with
  | mk v hv =>
    cases v with
    | zero =>
      Hint "Here `i = 0` and `![1, 2, 0] 0 = 1`. Prove `1 /= 0`."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h; cases h`"
      intro h; cases h
    | succ n =>
      cases n with
      | zero =>
        Hint "Here `i = 1` and `![1, 2, 0] 1 = 2`. Prove `2 /= 1`."
        Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h; cases h`"
        intro h; cases h
      | succ m =>
        cases m with
        | zero =>
          Hint "Here `i = 2` and `![1, 2, 0] 2 = 0`. Prove `0 /= 2`."
          Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h; cases h`"
          intro h; cases h
        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
The cyclic permutation `![1, 2, 0]` sends every element to a
different element — it has no fixed points. A permutation with
no fixed points is called a **derangement**. Derangements appear
throughout combinatorics — counting them leads to beautiful
formulas involving alternating sums and factorials.

This is your first encounter with `Fin n -> Fin n` as an
algebraic object. In later worlds, you'll see that:
- Permutations form a **group** under composition
- The number of permutations of `Fin n` is $n!$ (factorial)
- The number of derangements of `Fin n` is approximately $n!/e$

The tools you already have — case analysis, inequality, function
extensionality — are exactly what you need to prove properties
of concrete permutations.
"

World "PsetFin"
Level 15

Title "Permutation Algebra"

Introduction "
# Composition and Inverse

In Level 14, you proved that the cyclic permutation `![1, 2, 0]`
has no fixed points. Now you'll study its **algebraic structure**:
what happens when you compose it with itself?

**Part 1 — Composition**: Applying the cyclic permutation twice gives
the **reverse cycle** `![2, 0, 1]`:
- `0 -> 1 -> 2`
- `1 -> 2 -> 0`
- `2 -> 0 -> 1`

**Part 2 — Inverse**: The reverse cycle `![2, 0, 1]` is the
**inverse** of `![1, 2, 0]` — composing them gives the identity:
- `0 -> 1 -> 0`
- `1 -> 2 -> 1`
- `2 -> 0 -> 2`

Each element returns to itself!

**Strategy** for both parts: `ext`, unfold with `Function.comp_apply`,
case-split on the index, and close with `rfl`.
"

/-- The square of the cyclic permutation and its inverse property. -/
Statement : ((![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 → Fin 3) ∘ (![1, 2, 0]) = ![2, 0, 1]) ∧
            ((![2, 0, 1] : Fin 3 → Fin 3) ∘ (![1, 2, 0]) = id) := by
  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: Composition gives reverse cycle
  · Hint "Part 1: Prove the square is `![2, 0, 1]`.
    Use `ext` to reduce to pointwise equality, then unfold
    the composition with `Function.comp_apply` and case-split."
    ext ⟨v, hv⟩
    Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Function.comp_apply]` unfolds the
    composition. Then case-split on `v`."
    rw [Function.comp_apply]
    cases v with
    | zero =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl` — at index 0: `![1,2,0] (![1,2,0] 0) = ![1,2,0] 1 = 2`."
      rfl
    | succ n =>
      cases n with
      | zero =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl` — at index 1: `![1,2,0] (![1,2,0] 1) = ![1,2,0] 2 = 0`."
        rfl
      | succ m =>
        cases m with
        | zero =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl` — at index 2: `![1,2,0] (![1,2,0] 2) = ![1,2,0] 0 = 1`."
          rfl
        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
  -- Part 2: Reverse cycle is the inverse
  · Hint "Part 2: Prove `![2, 0, 1]` composed with `![1, 2, 0]` is `id`.
    Same pattern: `ext`, unfold, case-split."
    ext ⟨v, hv⟩
    Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Function.comp_apply]` then case-split."
    rw [Function.comp_apply]
    cases v with
    | zero =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl` — `![2,0,1] (![1,2,0] 0) = ![2,0,1] 1 = 0 = id 0`."
      rfl
    | succ n =>
      cases n with
      | zero =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl` — `![2,0,1] (![1,2,0] 1) = ![2,0,1] 2 = 1 = id 1`."
        rfl
      | succ m =>
        cases m with
        | zero =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl` — `![2,0,1] (![1,2,0] 2) = ![2,0,1] 0 = 2 = id 2`."
          rfl
        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
You've proved both that the cyclic permutation squared gives
`![2, 0, 1]`, and that `![2, 0, 1]` is its **inverse**: composing
them gives the identity.

This is a surprising fact about order-3 elements: the square of an
order-3 permutation IS its inverse. Why? If `f^3 = id`, then
`f^2 . f = id`, so `f^2 = f^(-1)`. Squaring an order-3 element
doesn't move you further from the identity — it brings you one
step closer.

In group theory, this generalizes: for any element `g` of order `n`,
`g^(-1) = g^(n-1)`. You'll verify the order-3 fact directly in the
next level.
"

Title "The Cyclic Permutation Has Order 3"

Introduction "
# Proving Order 3 Directly

In Level 15, you proved `![1,2,0]^2 = ![2,0,1]` and that
`![2,0,1]` is the inverse (composing it with `![1,2,0]` gives `id`).

Now prove the punchline directly: applying `![1,2,0]` **three
times** returns to the identity.

The composition `![1,2,0] comp ![1,2,0] comp ![1,2,0]` evaluates as:
- At 0: `0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 0`
- At 1: `1 -> 2 -> 0 -> 1`
- At 2: `2 -> 0 -> 1 -> 2`

Every element returns to itself after three steps.

**Strategy**: `ext`, then `rw [Function.comp_apply, Function.comp_apply]`
to unfold both layers of composition, then case-split and close with `rfl`.

   522→# Finset.range 0 is Empty
   523→
   524→In MeetFin, you proved that `Fin 0` has no elements — no natural
   525→number is less than 0. The same fact holds for `Finset.range 0`:
   526→it contains no members, because `Finset.range 0` is the set of

   529→Level 4's conclusion mentioned this boundary case. Now you'll prove

   534→2. `intro h` — assume `h : x ∈ Finset.range 0`
   535→3. `rw [Finset.mem_range] at h` — converts to `h : x < 0`
   536→4. `omega` — contradiction (no natural number is less than 0)

   540→Statement : ∀ x : ℕ, x ∉ Finset.range 0 := by

   542→  intro x h
   543→  Hint "Now rewrite the membership: `rw [Finset.mem_range] at h`.
   544→  This gives `h : x < 0`."
   545→  rw [Finset.mem_range] at h
   546→  Hint "No natural number is less than 0. `omega` sees the
   547→  contradiction."
   548→  omega

   550→Conclusion "
   551→`Finset.range 0` is empty — it has no members. This directly
   552→parallels `Fin 0` being empty (MeetFin Level 13): both facts come

   558→| `i.isLt` gives `i.val < 0` — impossible | `mem_range` gives `x < 0` — impossible |

   561→range is empty. For `n >= 1`, the range has `n` elements:

   799→# Proving Non-membership in a Literal Finset
   800→
   801→In Level 5, you proved `5 ∉ Finset.range 5` by assuming membership
   802→and reaching a contradiction. The same pattern works for literal
   803→finsets.
   804→
   805→To prove `4 ∉ {1, 2, 3}`:
   806→1. `intro h` — assume `h : 4 ∈ {1, 2, 3}`
   807→2. Unfold `h` completely: `rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert,
   808→   Finset.mem_singleton] at h` — this converts `h` to
   809→   `4 = 1 ∨ 4 = 2 ∨ 4 = 3`
   810→3. `omega` — all three disjuncts are false, so `omega` derives `False`
   811→
   812→**Chained rewrites in a hypothesis**: You can chain multiple rewrites
   813→in a single `rw [...] at h` — the same syntax you used for chained
   814→rewrites on goals. This peels all the `insert`/`singleton` layers
   815→at once.
   816→
   817→**Why omega works on disjunctions**: `omega` reasons about linear
   818→arithmetic over all hypotheses simultaneously. When every branch
   819→of a disjunction leads to a false arithmetic statement, `omega`
   820→sees the overall contradiction.
   821→"
   822→
   823→/-- 4 is not an element of {1, 2, 3}. -/
   824→Statement : (4 : ℕ) ∉ ({1, 2, 3} : Finset ℕ) := by
   825→  Hint "Assume membership: `intro h`."
   826→  intro h
   827→  Hint "Now unfold `{h}` completely. Chain the rewrites:
   828→  `rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h`."
   829→  Hint (hidden := true) "After the chained rewrite, `{h}` becomes
   830→  `4 = 1 ∨ 4 = 2 ∨ 4 = 3`. Then `omega` closes the goal."
   831→  rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h
   832→  Hint "Now `{h}` says `4 = 1 ∨ 4 = 2 ∨ 4 = 3`. All branches are
   833→  false. `omega` derives the contradiction."
   834→  omega
   835→
   836→Conclusion "
   837→Non-membership in a literal finset follows the same pattern as
   838→non-membership in a range:
   839→
   840→| Step | Range | Literal finset |
   841→|---|---|---|
   842→| Assume | `intro h` | `intro h` |
   843→| Unfold | `rw [mem_range] at h` | `rw [mem_insert, ..., mem_singleton] at h` |
   844→| Contradict | `omega` | `omega` |
   845→
   846→The only difference is *how many* rewrites are needed. For a finset
   847→with $k$ elements written as `insert a₁ (insert a₂ (... {aₖ}))`,
   848→you need $k - 1$ `mem_insert` rewrites and one `mem_singleton` at
   849→the end.
   850→
   851→You now have both membership and non-membership proofs for
   852→literal finsets.
   853→"
   854→

   856→import Game.Metadata

   858→World "FinsetBasics"
   859→Level 12
   860→
   861→Title "Reading Membership"
   862→
   863→Introduction "
   864→# Reading Membership
   865→
   866→So far, you've been *proving* membership: showing that a specific
   867→element belongs to a finset. But sometimes you start with a
   868→membership hypothesis and need to extract information from it.
   869→
   870→If you know `h : x ∈ {1, 2, 3}`, what can you conclude about `x`?
   871→Since `{1, 2, 3} = insert 1 (insert 2 {3})`, you can rewrite `h`
   872→using the same membership lemmas — but at the hypothesis instead
   873→of the goal:
   874→
   875→```
   876→rw [Finset.mem_insert] at h    -- h : x = 1 ∨ x ∈ {2, 3}
   877→rw [Finset.mem_insert] at h    -- h : x = 1 ∨ x = 2 ∨ x ∈ {3}
   878→rw [Finset.mem_singleton] at h -- h : x = 1 ∨ x = 2 ∨ x = 3
   879→```
   880→
   881→You can chain all three in one step:
   882→`rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h`
   883→
   884→This transforms a membership hypothesis into an explicit disjunction
   885→listing the possible values of `x`. This is the **backward** direction
   886→of finset membership — the counterpart to the forward direction you've
   887→been practicing.
   888→
   889→**Your task**: Given `h : x ∈ {1, 2, 3}`, prove `x = 1 ∨ x = 2 ∨ x = 3`.
   890→"
   891→
   892→/-- Membership in a literal finset means equality with one of the elements. -/
   893→Statement (x : ℕ) (h : x ∈ ({1, 2, 3} : Finset ℕ)) : x = 1 ∨ x = 2 ∨ x = 3 := by
   894→  Hint "Unfold the membership in `h` using
   895→  `rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h`."
   896→  rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h
   897→  Hint "Now `h` states exactly `x = 1 ∨ x = 2 ∨ x = 3`, which matches
   898→  the goal. Close it with `exact h`."
   899→  exact h
   900→
   901→Conclusion "
   902→You just used `Finset.mem_insert` in the **backward** direction:
   903→starting from a membership hypothesis and unfolding it into a
   904→disjunction of equalities.
   905→
   906→| Direction | What you have | What you do |
   907→|---|---|---|
   908→| Forward (proving) | Goal: `x ∈ {{a, b, c}}` | `rw [mem_insert]` at goal, then `left`/`right` |
   909→| Backward (reading) | Hyp: `h : x ∈ {{a, b, c}}` | `rw [mem_insert, ...] at h`, then use `h` |
   910→
   911→This backward direction is essential for the next world. When you
   912→need to prove a subset relation `s ⊆ t` (meaning `∀ x ∈ s, x ∈ t`),
   913→you'll start with a membership hypothesis `h : x ∈ s` and need to
   914→show `x ∈ t` — exactly the pattern you just practiced.
   915→"

   919→import Game.Metadata
   920→
   921→World "FinsetBasics"
   922→Level 13
   923→
   924→Title "Insert is Idempotent"
   925→
   926→Introduction "
   927→# Insert is Idempotent
   928→
   929→Here's a common misconception: inserting an element always makes the
   930→set bigger. **Wrong.** Finsets have no duplicates, so inserting an
   931→element that's already present does nothing.
   932→
   933→The formal statement is `Finset.insert_eq_of_mem`:
   934→
   935→$$a \\in s \\;\\longrightarrow\\; \\texttt{insert } a \\; s = s$$
   936→
   937→Notice this is an **implication**, not an iff. The direction matters:
   938→- If `a ∈ s`, then `insert a s = s` (idempotent)
   939→- If `a ∉ s`, then `insert a s ≠ s` (the set grows by one element)
   940→
   941→To use `insert_eq_of_mem`, you `apply` it. This changes the goal from
   942→an equation (`insert a s = s`) to a membership proof (`a ∈ s`). Then
   943→you prove membership using the `mem_insert`/`mem_singleton` chain
   944→from earlier levels.
   945→
   946→**Your task**: Prove that `insert 2 {1, 2, 3} = {1, 2, 3}`.
   947→"
   948→
   949→/-- Inserting 2 into {1, 2, 3} gives back {1, 2, 3}. -/
   950→Statement : insert 2 ({1, 2, 3} : Finset ℕ) = {1, 2, 3} := by
   951→  Hint "Use `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` to reduce the equation to
   952→  a membership proof."
   953→  apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem
   954→  Hint "Now prove that `2` is a member of the set. Peel the inserts:
   955→  `rw [Finset.mem_insert]`, then `right`,
   956→  then `rw [Finset.mem_insert]`, then `left; rfl`."
   957→  Hint (hidden := true) "First `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` gives a disjunction
   958→  `2 = 1 ∨ ...`. Take `right`. Then `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` gives
   959→  `2 = 2 ∨ ...`. Take `left; rfl`."
   960→  rw [Finset.mem_insert]
   961→  right
   962→  rw [Finset.mem_insert]
   963→  left
   964→  rfl
   965→
   966→Conclusion "
   967→The proof has two stages:
   968→1. `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` — reduce the equation to membership
   969→2. Prove membership using the `mem_insert`/`mem_singleton` chain
   970→
   971→This is the **apply-then-prove** pattern: use `apply` with a theorem
   972→whose conclusion matches the goal, and the theorem's premise becomes
   973→your new goal. Here, `apply insert_eq_of_mem` matches the goal
   974→`insert a s = s` and leaves the premise `a ∈ s` to prove.
   975→
   976→This pattern recurs throughout mathematics in Lean. Whenever a
   977→theorem's conclusion matches your goal, `apply` it — and the premise
   978→becomes your new goal. Watch for this pattern in future worlds.
   979→
   980→In plain mathematics, $\\{2\\} \\cup \\{1, 2, 3\\} = \\{1, 2, 3\\}$ is
   981→obvious because 2 is already in the set. In Lean, you must *prove*
   982→the membership to justify the equality.
   983→"
   984→
   985→/-- `Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` states that if `a ∈ s`, then
   986→`insert a s = s`.
   987→
   988→Inserting an element that's already present is a no-op.
   989→
   990→## Syntax
   991→```
   992→apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem
   993→```
   994→This changes the goal from `insert a s = s` to `a ∈ s`.
   995→
   996→## When to use it
   997→When you need to prove that inserting a duplicate element
   998→doesn't change the finset.
   999→

2. `rw [... ] at h` — unfold to something concrete
3. `omega` — derive a contradiction

This is **proof by contradiction** in a very targeted form: you don't
need to invoke any fancy contradiction tactic — just show that the
hypothesis leads to a false arithmetic statement.

This pattern extends beyond `range`. In the next level, you'll use
the same technique to prove that `Finset.range 0` is empty — the
ultimate boundary case.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetBasics"
Level 6

Title "The Empty Range"

Introduction "
# Finset.range 0 is Empty

In MeetFin, you proved that `Fin 0` has no elements — no natural
number is less than 0. The same fact holds for `Finset.range 0`:
it contains no members, because `Finset.range 0` is the set of
natural numbers less than 0.

Level 4's conclusion mentioned this boundary case. Now you'll prove
it: no natural number belongs to `Finset.range 0`.

**Strategy**: The non-membership pattern from Level 5:
1. `intro x` — introduce the element
2. `intro h` — assume `h : x ∈ Finset.range 0`
3. `rw [Finset.mem_range] at h` — converts to `h : x < 0`
4. `omega` — contradiction (no natural number is less than 0)
"

/-- No natural number belongs to Finset.range 0. -/
Statement : ∀ x : ℕ, x ∉ Finset.range 0 := by
  Hint "Introduce `x` and assume membership: `intro x h`."
  intro x h
  Hint "Now rewrite the membership: `rw [Finset.mem_range] at h`.
  This gives `h : x < 0`."
  rw [Finset.mem_range] at h
  Hint "No natural number is less than 0. `omega` sees the
  contradiction."
  omega

Conclusion "
`Finset.range 0` is empty — it has no members. This directly
parallels `Fin 0` being empty (MeetFin Level 13): both facts come
from the same arithmetic truth that no natural number is less than 0.

| Phase 1 | Phase 2 |
|---|---|
| `Fin 0` is empty (no inhabitants) | `Finset.range 0` is empty (no members) |
| `i.isLt` gives `i.val < 0` — impossible | `mem_range` gives `x < 0` — impossible |

This is the boundary case of `Finset.range n`. For `n = 0`, the
range is empty. For `n >= 1`, the range has `n` elements:
`{0, 1, ..., n-1}`.

The equality `Finset.range 0 = ∅` (which follows from what you just
proved) will be useful later when you encounter base cases of
induction on finset ranges.
"
